subject: logic
title: Exactly One Statement is True
question: Here are four statements. Exactly one of them is true. Which one?
answer: C - "Exactly three of these statements are false."
modelReasoningPath:
If exactly one statement is true, then exactly three are false. - Check C: "Exactly three of these statements are false" - yes, that is what we've assumed. C is true. ✓ - Check A: "Exactly one is false" - we have three false statements, so A is false. ✓ - Check B: "Exactly two are false" - false. ✓ - Check D: "Exactly four are false" - false (one is true). ✓ So C being the one true statement is self-consistent. No other choice works: - If A were the one true statement, then exactly one statement would be false - meaning three are true. But that contradicts A being the only true statement. - Similarly, B and D each contradict themselves when assumed to be the sole true statement.

What to listen for: The child should test whether each statement is consistent with being "the one true statement." A child who immediately says "C, because if exactly one is true then exactly three are false, and C says exactly that" has understood the self-referential structure instantly. More commonly, a child will need to be walked through testing A first - show them how the assumption collapses, then they can apply the same method to B, C, D. ---
